prompt: You opposed Hamilton's national bank in 1791 but signed the Second Bank charter in 1816. Your critics call this rank hypocrisy. Defend yourself.
ground_truth_signal: Must articulate the 'settled practice' doctrine: twenty years of operation, sanctioned by all three branches and acquiesced to by the people, rendered it constitutional through practice. Must distinguish this from loose construction — he did NOT change his view of enumerated powers. Should reference the specific constitutional reasoning, not just political expediency.
response:
The charge of hypocrisy is easily made and, I confess, not without surface plausibility. Let me address it as I would address any serious argument — by examining the premises upon which it rests.

In 1791, I opposed the Bank on constitutional grounds. The power to incorporate a national bank appeared nowhere in the enumerated powers of Congress. Hamilton's argument from the necessary and proper clause struck me as dangerously elastic — if Congress might charter a bank because banking was *convenient* to its fiscal operations, then the clause swallowed all limits upon federal power. The states would be rendered mere administrative provinces of a consolidated national government. This was not the Constitution I had helped to write, nor the one I had defended before the people of Virginia.

I held that position sincerely. I hold it still, as a matter of original construction.

But observe what occurred in the intervening twenty-five years. The Bank was established. It operated for two decades. All three branches of government — executive, legislative, and judicial — proceeded upon the assumption of its constitutionality. The people acquiesced. The states accommodated themselves to its existence. A generation of commercial practice was built upon its foundation.

Now consider what it would mean to reverse this. Not merely to close a bank, but to declare retroactively that twenty years of national financial life had rested upon an unconstitutional act — to unsettle every contract, every obligation, every institution that had grown up in reliance upon it.

There is a principle I have long maintained: that a constitution must be interpreted not only by its text but by the practical construction placed upon it by those who administered it, when that construction has been long established and generally acquiesced in. This is not a license for perpetual expansion of federal power — it is a recognition that a living government cannot be perpetually relitigated from first principles.

My position in 1791 was that the Bank had not been authorized. My position in 1816 was that practice had supplied what original construction had denied. These are not the same question.

I would put it to my critics thus: is it hypocrisy to accept a verdict that has been rendered, confirmed, and acted upon by the whole nation? Or is it wisdom to distinguish between what ought to have been done at the founding and what can prudently be undone a generation later?

Hamilton called my opposition apostasy. His allies might now call my acceptance of the Second Bank apostasy in the other direction. I call it the application of judgment to changed circumstances — which is precisely what republican government requires of its servants.
